title: Boulangère potatoes
subtitle: Sliced potatoes baked in stock beneath the roast
excerpt: Thinly sliced potatoes and onions layered in a tin and baked slowly in stock until the top is golden and the layers beneath have absorbed the broth into something soft and savoury. Leaner than gratin dauphinois, and better for catching the juices from a leg of lamb or shoulder of pork.
sourceNotes: Classic French dish carried into the British repertoire by Elizabeth David in French Provincial Cooking (1960). The name refers to the village baker's oven, where the dish was traditionally brought to cook in the residual heat after the bread was drawn. Method draws on the tradition documented by David and by Jane Grigson in Good Things (1971).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 24 hours ahead, cover, and refrigerate. Add 10 minutes to the baking time from cold.

content.text: Boulangère potatoes are the leaner cousin of gratin dauphinois: no cream, no cheese, just potato, onion, stock, and thyme. What the dish sacrifices in richness it gains in absorption — the stock reduces into the potato layers as they cook, producing something deeply savoury that pairs better with roast meat than a cream-heavy gratin. It is particularly good placed directly beneath a leg of lamb so the meat juices drip into the tin during the final part of the roast.

Slice the potatoes thinly (2 to 3 mm) on a mandoline if you have one, or with a sharp knife and patience. Floury potatoes such as Maris Piper or King Edward break down slightly during the long cook and meld into the layers; waxy varieties hold their shape more firmly, which is also acceptable but gives a different texture.

items.prepNote: Maris Piper, about 1.2 kg total, peeled and thinly sliced

content.text: Preheat the oven to 170°C fan. Butter a rectangular baking tin generously. Arrange a single layer of potato slices across the base, slightly overlapping. Scatter over some sliced onion, garlic, thyme leaves, and season well. Repeat the layers (potato, onion, garlic, herb, seasoning) until all the {{potato}} potato slices and {{onion}} onions are used. Finish with a neat top layer of overlapping potato.

Tuck the bay leaves down the sides and pour {{stock-chicken}} of hot chicken stock over the layers; it should come about three-quarters up the depth of the tin. Dot the surface with {{unsalted-butter}} of unsalted butter in small pieces. Bake uncovered for 1 hour 30 minutes until the top is golden brown and a knife slides through the layers without resistance. If the top colours too quickly, cover loosely with foil and continue.

items.symptom: Layers are still firm after the full baking time
items.cause: Slices were cut too thickly, or the oven temperature was too low
items.fix: Return to the oven for another 20 minutes with foil on top. Thin slicing is the single most important factor for even cooking.

items.symptom: Top has dried out and crisped before the middle is cooked
items.cause: Stock evaporated too fast; oven too hot or not enough liquid
items.fix: Cover with foil and pour a little extra hot stock around the edges.

items.cause: Insufficient seasoning between layers, or weak stock
items.fix: Season each layer of potato individually. The stock should taste well-seasoned before it goes in.

items.cause: Too much stock, or the tin was covered during baking
items.fix: Bake uncovered throughout so the stock reduces into the layers rather than steaming the potatoes.

content.text: The name translates as 'baker's wife's potatoes', from the custom of bringing the assembled dish to the village baker to cook in his oven after the bread was drawn — the residual heat was sufficient for a long, slow braise. Elizabeth David introduced the dish to British readers in French Provincial Cooking (1960), and it became a regular part of the British Sunday-lunch canon through the 1970s. In the Périgord region of France it is traditionally placed beneath a roasting leg of lamb so that the meat drips directly into the potato layers during cooking, which is worth doing if your oven can accommodate the arrangement.
